request.question: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?

title: Red Rag Top
Angry All the Time
Watch the Wind Blow By
Tim McGraw (song)
Honky Cat
Emotional Traffic
Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters
Tiny Dancer
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
One of These Days (Marcus Hummon song)
sentences: "Red Rag Top" is a song written by Jason White and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in September 2002 as the first single from his album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song peaked at number 5 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart in early 2003 and peaked at number 40 on the U.S. "Billboard" Hot 100 chart.
"Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.
Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.
The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
"Watch the Wind Blow By" is a song written by Anders Osborne and Dylan Altman, and performed by American country music singer Tim McGraw.
It was released in October 2003 as the fifth and final single from McGraw's album "Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors".
The song reached the top of the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks chart on March 20, 2004.
It also peaked at number 32 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
"Tim McGraw" is the debut single and first published song recorded by American singer-songwriter Taylor Swift.
The song was written by Swift and Liz Rose, and produced by Nathan Chapman.
It was released on June 19, 2006 by Big Machine Records as Swift's debut single and the lead single from Swift's eponymous debut album.
Swift wrote "Tim McGraw" during her freshman year of high school, knowing that she and her senior boyfriend would break up at the end of the year when he left for college.
The song was written about all the different things that would remind the subject of Swift and their time spent together, once he departed.
"Tim McGraw" is a musical interconnection of traditional and modern country music.
Lyrically, the track lists items in order to associate a past relationship, one of them being country artist Tim McGraw's music.
"Honky Cat" is a song from the 1972 Elton John album "Honky Château," the album's lead-off track.
Emotional Traffic is the eleventh studio album by American country music artist Tim McGraw.
Originally completed in late 2010, it is McGraw's last studio album released by Curb Records, a label he's been with since his debut album "Tim McGraw" (1993).
It was released on January 24, 2012.
"Mona Lisas and Mad Hatters" is a song from the Elton John album "Honky Château".
The lyrics were written by Bernie Taupin and is his take on New York City after hearing a gun go off near his hotel window during his first visit to the city.
The song's lyrics were partly inspired by Ben E. King's "Spanish Harlem," written by Jerry Leiber and Phil Spector, in which he sings "There is a rose in Spanish Harlem."

In response to this, Taupin writes, Allmusic critic Stewart Mason noted that the song is "less saccharine than many similar Elton John and Bernie Taupin ballads" and praised the "somewhat uncharacteristic emotional directness" of its lyrics.
It was released as the B-side of the "Harmony" U.K. single in 1980.
"Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.
It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.
In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.
In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.
Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.
It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.
Four singles were released.
Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.
The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.
The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000
"One of These Days" is a song co-written and originally recorded by American country music singer Marcus Hummon on his 1995 album "All in Good Time".
It was later covered by Tim McGraw, whose version is the fourth single from his 1997 album "Everywhere".
It peaked at number two in the United States, and number one in Canada.

title: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors
Tiny Dancer
Tiny Dancer

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: What Elton John album included a song that was later covered in 2002 by Tim McGraw?
hop1_docs: Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Let It Go (Tim McGraw album) | Let It Go is the ninth studio album by Tim McGraw.  Released on March 27, 2007, it was his first studio album in two and a half years.  "Let It Go" entered the U.S. "Billboard" 200 at number one with sales of 325,000.  The album has produced seven Top 20 singles on the "Billboard" Hot Country Songs charts, including a number one; one of those seven songs was only included on later issues of the album.  Of all McGraw's albums, this one has produced the most singles in his career.

Tim McGraw (album) | Tim McGraw is the first album by American country music artist Tim McGraw, released in 1993.  It includes the singles "What Room Was the Holiday In", "Welcome to the Club", "Two Steppin' Mind", and "Memory Lane", none of which reached the Top 40 on the country charts.  This is the only studio album of McGraw's career not to achieve a music recording sales certification or to enter the Top Country Albums charts.

Angry All the Time | "Angry All the Time" is a song written by Bruce Robison and first recorded on his 1998 album "Wrapped".  It was later covered by Tim McGraw with guest vocalist Faith Hill.  Released in July 2001, McGraw's version was the second single from his "Set This Circus Down" album.  The song reached Number One on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) chart.
Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida | Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida is a 1999 concept album that contains songs from, but predates the main production of, the 2000 musical "Aida".

Unbroken (Tim McGraw song) | "Unbroken" is a song written by Annie Roboff and Holly Lamar, and recorded by American country music artist Tim McGraw.  It was released in May 2002 as the fourth and final single from his album "Set This Circus Down".  The song reached No. 1 on the "Billboard" Hot Country Singles & Tracks (now Hot Country Songs) charts.

Telluride (song) | "Telluride" is a song originally recorded by Tim McGraw on his 2001 album "Set This Circus Down".  McGraw's version, though never a single, charted twice on Hot Country Songs as an album cut.  It reached No. 52 on its first entry, and then re-entered at No. 59 in 2002.

Elton John and Tim Rice's Aida
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- Tim McGraw: Passage [1] (Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors) — quoted facts: "released in November 2002" and "included a cover of Elton John's 'Tiny Dancer'".
- Elton John: No passage here states which Elton John album contains "Tiny Dancer" (no Elton John album title is given in the provided passages).

2) Ambiguities:
- Only one Elton John song is cited as covered in 2002 ("Tiny Dancer" in [1]); no other Elton John songs or candidate covers appear in the passages.
- There is no candidate Elton John album title present in the passages; multiple Elton John albums could potentially contain the song (not listed).

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity (as it appears in the passages): "Tiny Dancer".
- Precise missing fact needed: the exact Elton John album title (and optionally release year/track placement) that originally included "Tiny Dancer".

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Open the "Tiny Dancer" (song) page — check the lede/infobox and "Release" or "Album appearance" / "Composition" sections for "original album" and year. Search keywords: Tiny Dancer Elton John album.
- If that page is missing, open Elton John's main page or "Elton John discography" — check album track listings for the album that lists "Tiny Dancer".
- As a fallback, fetch likely Elton John album pages and their track listings (check lede/infobox and "Track listing" sections) until the album containing "Tiny Dancer" is confirmed.

Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer) | "Tiny Dancer (Hold Me Closer)" is a song by Ironik, from his debut album "No Point in Wasting Tears", released on 11 May 2009.  The song heavily samples British singer Elton John's 1972 single "Tiny Dancer".  This is the first single by Ironik to be released without a maxi single.

Tiny Dancer | "Tiny Dancer" is a 1971 song by Elton John with lyrics by Bernie Taupin.  It appears on John's fourth album, "Madman Across the Water", and was released as a single in 1972.  In the U.S. it was certified Gold on 19 May 2005, and Platinum on 19 August 2011 by the Recording Industry Association of America.  In the UK, "Tiny Dancer" has been certified Silver by the British Phonographic Industry (BPI) for sales of 200,000 copies, despite never being released as an official single.

Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors | Tim McGraw and the Dancehall Doctors is the seventh studio album by country musician Tim McGraw and the first to feature his band The Dancehall Doctors.  It was released in November 2002 and was recorded on a mountaintop studio in upstate New York.  Four singles were released.  Two songs were in the movie "Black Cloud", starring McGraw.  The album also included a cover of Elton John's "Tiny Dancer", which was released only to the AC format, although it also reached the country charts from unsolicited airplay.  The album debuted at number 2 on the Billboard 200 with first week sales of 602,000

Elton John: The Classic Years | Elton John: The Classic Years (known in North America as Elton John: The Remasters) was a remastering series of Sir Elton John's albums, in which some of the albums included bonus tracks.

Elton John albums discography | The albums discography of English musician/singer-songwriter Elton John consists of 30 studio albums, 4 live albums, 7 soundtrack albums, 16 compilation albums and 3 extended plays, as well as 2 other albums.  John has sold more than 200 million albums worldwide.  Elton John, is The Highest Selling British Solo Artist of All Time

Elton John's Greatest Hits Vol. 3 | Elton John’s Greatest Hits Volume III is the twenty-seventh album release for Elton John.  Released in 1987, 10 years after "Elton John's Greatest Hits Volume II", the compilation album features his greatest hits from 1979–1986 and was made available only in the United States and Canada.  All of the songs featured had previously been released on a previous album.

The Fox (Elton John album) | The Fox, released in 1981, is the fifteenth official album release for Elton John.  With sales of approx. 300,000 US copies, it was one of Elton's poorest-selling albums in the United States.
